narrative: As a white male applicant with a high family income bracket, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting the likelihood of passing on the first try based on academic and background factors. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.60, LSAT score of 32, Year 1 grade decile of 3, Year 3 grade decile of 2, and full-time enrollment status—and calculated a 54.6% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This tipped the scales just over the threshold, leading to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to succeed on the bar. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 grade decile of 2, which is notably lower than the typical student's decile of about 5.5, signaling to the model a significant dip in performance that often correlates with bar struggles. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.60, while below the average of 3.21, worked against a failure prediction by showing some baseline academic capability compared to weaker profiles. Similarly, your high family income provided a counterbalancing influence, as students from similar backgrounds tend to have advantages that help them pass more often. Your LSAT score of 32, lower than the average of 36.7, and other details like full-time status played supporting roles but weren't the top drivers here. Compared to typical admitted students, who often have stronger mid-to-late academic deciles and higher test scores, your profile stood out for that Year 3 weakness, which outweighed the positives from your GPA and family support. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a stable background—many in similar situations succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, focus on boosting your grades through tutoring or study habits to climb those deciles, retake the LSAT for a higher score, and consider part-time options if full-time work might dilute your focus. Building a stronger narrative around your resilience, perhaps with letters explaining any Year 3 challenges, could help in reapplying to other schools. With deliberate steps, you can shift your profile toward the passing predictions that open doors.
